data.title: Palm Oil Edges Higher Amid Soybean Oil Strength, Bargain Hunting — Market Talk
data.description: Palm oil ended slightly higher amid soybean oil's gains overnight and bargain hunting activities after a recent slump. Palm oil rose after the strong recovery seen in rival soybean oil, which erased most of its earlier losses, Kenanga Futures said in a research note. However, gains were likely limited by a firmer Malaysia ringgit, which could dampen export competitiveness, it added. Kenanga sets palm oil's support and resistance levels at 3,780 ringgit and 3,950 ringgit, respectively. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for August delivery ended 7 ringgit higher at 3,827 ringgit a ton. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

data.title: Canada Post Warns of Possible Delays as Union Imposes Overtime Ban
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart

OTTAWA--Canada's postal agency warned of possible delays and a further hit to its already-struggling operations as unionized workers halted overtime but remained on the job for now.

The Canadian Union of Postal Workers, which represents more than 55,000 Canada Post employees across the country, on Friday launched a ban on overtime for tens of thousands of mail carriers coast to coast who are seeking a new collective deal.

The move will mean unionized workers won't work any more than eight hours a day and will refuse to work more than 40 hours a week.

The dispute, and the possibility of a labor stoppage similar to a one-month strike late last year that halted almost all deliveries, comes as Canada's economy is already under pressure and on the cusp of a possible recession due to trade tensions with the U.S. and the sweeping tariffs imposed by the Trump administration.

The union on Monday issued a strike notice to Canada Post, which days later presented a fresh offer to urban as well as rural and suburban mail carriers that included protections for existing benefits and improved wage increases.

The union in a letter to members said it decided to proceed with an overtime ban to minimize both disruptions to the public and lost work days for members, but it held out the prospect of escalating its actions if Canada Post changes working conditions, suspends benefits or begin layoffs.

Union officials during the week had offered the government-owned agency a two-week truce to continue negotiation, which the company declined.

Canada Post said that with the overtime ban it will continue operating, but that customers may experience delays. It said it was unclear how the postal union's strike action might evolve, though for now there are no rotating strikes and there is no national work stoppage.

The agency warned it has already seen parcel and mail volumes decline significantly as customers prepare for another potential labor disruption and either seek out alternative services or halt sending mail or packages for fear these might get frozen in Canada Post's network if workers walk off the job.

Efforts to secure a fresh collective deal with tens of thousands of mail carriers come at a critical time for Canada Post, which is looking to make changes to its delivery model to breathe life into its finances. The agency has warned it expects to report a seventh consecutive annual loss for 2024.

The federal government in December forced an end to a month-long strike by postal workers, and it extended collective agreements for mail carriers until Thursday to allow more time to reach a settlement and to resume deliveries of mail and parcels.

Canada Post suspended negotiations last week after little headway was made during several days of talks in late April and early May.

Under its latest offer, mail carriers would receive wage increases of 6% in the first year, 3% in year two and 2% in each of the third and fourth years. Current employees would keep their defined benefit pension, job-security provisions, health benefits, vacation and other entitlements. It no longer is proposing a new health-benefits plan, changes to workers' post-retirement benefits or enrollment of new employees in the defined contribution pension.

Still, Canada Post is proposing parcel delivery seven days a week and is looking to turn to part-time workers, a sticking point with the union. The postal agency said part-time positions would be provided with health and pension benefits, as well as scheduled and guaranteed hours.

Canada Post also is proposing an initial, limited use of so-called dynamic routing, which would allow it to change routes daily to make more efficient use of workers.

A report released last week by an industrial commission set up by Ottawa said Canada Post faced an "existential crisis," and that immediate changes were needed or its fiscal situation would continue to deteriorate. It recommended an end to door-to-door mail delivery to homes but said daily delivery to businesses should be maintained. It also suggested the company be allowed to hire part-time employees to work weekends and possibly during the week, though it said these workers should be paid the same as full-time employees and receive the same benefits.

The union in response to the commission's report said it was positive that the commission concluded Canada Post should continue to exist as a public service, but it criticised the report as skewed in favor of Canada Post's positions and recommendations. It said the recommendations amount to service cuts, contracting out, and major rollbacks of provisions in collective agreements.

During last year's strike, processing and delivery of mail and parcels, except for some government social-assistance checks, was stopped and the company issued a lockout notice for unionized workers. That shutdown hit postal services in the busy online sales period heading into Christmas, and disrupted small businesses that rely on Canada Post deliveries.

Write to Robb M. Stewart at robb.stewart@wsj.com

data.title: Prostatype Genomics’ CEO interviewed by Aktiespararna and Redeye during ongoing rights issue
data.description:
The subscription period in Prostatype Genomics AB's (“Prostatype Genomics” or the “Company”) rights issue of units (shares and warrants of series TO5) with preferential rights for the Company's existing shareholders (the “Rights Issue”) commenced on May 21, 2025, and will end on June 4, 2025. Through the Rights Issue, which is secured to approximately 81 percent, the Company can receive an initial maximum of approximately SEK 27.3 million, which is intended to finance the completion of the US validation study and the Medicare approval process as well as ongoing commercialization activities in the US and Europe. The Company's CEO, Fredrik Rickman, has been interviewed by Aktiespararna and Redeye in the past week. These interviews are available via the links below.

Interview with Aktiespararna

Prostatype Genomics CEO Fredrik Rickman is interviewed by Aktiespararna. Click on the link below to access the interview.

Aktiespararna – interview with Prostatype Genomics 21 May 2025

Interview with Redeye

Prostatype Genomics CEO Fredrik Rickman is interviewed by Redeye. Click on the link below to access the interview.

Redeye – interview with Prostatype Genomics 23 May 2025

Summary of the Rights Issue

  • Existing shareholders have received one (1) unit right for each share held on the record date 19 May 2025. One (1) unit right entitles the holder to subscribe for one (1) unit in the Company.
  • Each unit consists of four (4) new shares and three (3) warrants of series TO5.
  • The subscription price in the Rights Issue is SEK 4.00 per unit, corresponding to SEK 1.00 per share. warrants of series TO5 are issued free of charge.
  • Upon full subscription in the Rights Issue, Prostatype Genomics will initially receive approximately SEK 27.3 million before deduction of issue costs.
  • The subscription period in the Rights Issue runs until and including 4 June 2025. Please note that different banks apply different closing dates for the subscription period, so shareholders are advised to check with their bank for the specific closing date.
  • The Rights Issue is covered by pre-subscription commitments and guarantee commitments of a total of approximately 81 percent. The guarantee commitments consist partly of so-called top-down underwritings and partly of so-called bottom underwritings.
  • One (1) warrant of series TO5 entitles the holder to subscribe for one (1) new share in the Company during the period 3 September 2025 until and including 17 September 2025 at an exercise price of 30% discount of the 20-day volume-weighted average price prior to the first day of the exercise period. However, no less than SEK 0.10 and no higher than SEK 1.50 per share. Warrants of series TO5 are expected to be admitted to trading on Nasdaq First North Growth Market in close connection with the registration of the Rights Issue with the Swedish Companies Registration Office and the conversion of BTU to ordinary shares and warrants.
  • In order to meet the interest for participating in the Rights Issue and thereby further strengthening the Company’s financial position, Board has been authorized by the annual general meeting to issue additional units through so-called over-allotment in the event of oversubscription of the Rights Issue. The authorization is limited to approximately 15 percent of the total amount of units in the Rights Issue and an issue in accordance with the authorization shall be made on corresponding terms as the Rights Issue.

Time plan for the Rights Issue

  • 30 May 2025: trading in unit rights ends
  • 4 June 2025: subscription period ends
  • Around 5 June 2025: press release with outcome of Rights Issue is announced

Information to investors under the Screening of Foreign Direct Investment Act (2023:560)

As the Company conducts activities worthy of protection in accordance with the Screening of Foreign Direct Investment Act (2023:560), certain investments in the Rights Issue may require examination by the Inspectorate for Strategic Products. For more information, please visit the Swedish Inspectorate for Strategic Products' website, www.isp.se or contact the Company.

Advisors

Navia Corporate Finance AB and Gemstone Capital ApS are financial advisors and Bookrunners in connection with the Rights Issue. Advokatfirman Lindahl is the legal advisor. Nordic Issuing AB is the issuing agent.

For more information about the Rights Issue, please contact:

Navia Corporate Finance AB

E-mail: info@naviacf.se

Website: www.naviacorporatefinance.com

or

Gemstone Capital ApS

E-mail: info@gemstonecapital.com

Website: www.gemstonecapital.com

For more information about the Company, please contact:

Fredrik Rickman, CEO Prostatype Genomics AB

Telephone: +46 (0)73 049 77 01

E-mail: fredrik.rickman@prostatypegenomics.com

Certified Adviser

DNB Carnegie

Telephone: +46 (0)73 856 42 65

E-mail: certifiedadviser@carnegie.se

About Prostatype Genomics

Prostatype® is a genomic test that is available to patients and treating urologists as a complementary decision basis for the question of treatment or no-treatment of prostate cancer. The test was developed by a leading research group at Karolinska Institute and is provided by Prostatype Genomics AB. The Company’s Certified Adviser is DNB Carnegie.

data.title: Polyethylene terephthalate (PET) spot prices climb in Europe
data.description: This week, PET spot prices gained, while contract prices were left unchanged in the European region.An industry source in Europe informed a Polymerupdate team member, "The European spot market for polyethylene terephthalate showed a growing preference for virgin PET over recycled PET during the week, influenced by competitive offers of virgin material. Market players highlighted the existing price differential as a significant factor driving the consumption of virgin PET in Europe, with some manufacturers adapting to the prevailing demand levels. The demand trajectory aligns with forecasts and mirrors trends expected for 2024. Overall, the demand for preforms has not met expectations; however, the current pricing of virgin PET is prompting changes in consumer purchasing behavior among recyclers. Additionally, European market participants are closely observing global upstream markets, as rising prices for Asian paraxylene and constraints in the US monoethylene glycol market are anticipated to affect supply dynamics in Europe.In the spot markets, FD North West Europe PET prices were assessed at the Euro 955-965/mt levels, a rise of Euro (+10/mt) week on week. In the contract markets, bottle grade PET prices were assessed at the Euro 1075-1080/mt FD NWE Germany and FD NWE Italy levels, both unchanged week on week. PET bottle grade prices were assessed at the Euro 1075-1080/mt FD NWE France levels, rolled over from the previous week. Bottle grade PET prices were also assessed at the GBP 925-930/mt FD NWE UK levels, steady from last week.Feedstock MEG spot prices were assessed at the Euro 640-645/mt FD Northwest Europe levels, sharply lower by Euro (-40/mt) from the previous week, while PTA prices were assessed at the Euro 704-705/mt FD Northwest Europe levels, steeply higher by Euro (+35/mt) from last week.In Asia, PET prices were assessed at the USD 790-820/mt FOB North East Asia levels, stable from the previous week.

data.title: South African Rand at Over 5-Month High
data.description:
The South African rand appreciated toward 17.9 per USD, its highest since mid-December, buoyed by higher prices of precious metals, particularly gold and amid a weaker dollar.

Market sentiment was also shaped by the recent bilateral meeting between President Cyril Ramaphosa and President Trump, as well as developments surrounding the national budget.

Ramaphosa’s office described the U.S. visit as 'successful,' despite reported tensions during the White House meeting, where the two leaders reportedly agreed to initiate talks on critical minerals.

Meanwhile, Finance Minister Enoch Godongwana presented a third revision of the national budget on May 21, which gained support from both lawmakers and investors.

The previous two drafts failed to secure sufficient backing from within and outside the Government of National Unity (GNU), triggering an unprecedented budget crisis and raising concerns over the stability of the multiparty coalition.

data.title: Market Chatter: Deutsche Bank to Transfer Risk on $3 Billion Corporate Loan Book
data.description:
Deutsche Bank is looking to offload its risk tied to a portfolio of $3 billion corporate loans via a significant risk transfer deal, Bloomberg News reported Friday, citing people with knowledge of the matter.

The transfer is valued at about $240 million, or 8% of the loan pool, which includes assets from North America and Europe. The German Bank will issue the risk-transfer notes under its Craft program, the report said.

Deutsche Bank did not immediately respond to a request for comment from MT Newswires.

data.title: Market Chatter: Major US Banks Mulling Issuance of Joint Stablecoin
data.description:
Major US banks are in talks over their potential issuance of a joint stablecoin, which serves as a digital dollars in cryptocurrency markets, The Wall Street Journal reported Thursday, citing unnamed sources familiar with the matter.

Sources told the newspaper that talks have involved companies co-owned by Citigroup , Wells Fargo , Bank of America , JPMorgan Chase , and other major commercial banks so far. Discussions remain in early stages and the final decision would depend on legislation and other factors, the sources added.

Citigroup, Wells Fargo, Bank of America, and JPMorgan Chase did not immediately respond to MT Newswires' requests for comments.

data.title: Sterling Rises to 3-Year High Against Dollar — Market Talk
data.description: Sterling rises to a three-year high against the dollar, supported by a weaker dollar and Friday's stronger-than-expected U.K. retail sales data. The dollar falls on mounting concerns over ballooning U.S. government debt after the House passed President Trump's tax and spending bill. U.K. retail sales rose 1.2% month-on-month in April, beating the 0.3% rise forecast by analysts in a WSJ survey. The broad takeaway from the data is that households continue to spend and "that puts a floor under just how much U.K. growth can soften," Monex Europe analysts say in a note. Sterling rises to a high of $1.3502, according to FactSet, but is flat versus the euro, which last trades at 0.8409 pounds. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
Market Talks covering the impact of U.S. Politics and White House policies on companies and markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0616 ET - Sterling rises to a three-year high against the dollar, supported by a weaker dollar and Friday's stronger-than-expected U.K. retail sales data. The dollar falls on mounting concerns over ballooning U.S. government debt after the House passed President Trump's tax and spending bill. U.K. retail sales rose 1.2% month-on-month in April, beating the 0.3% rise forecast by analysts in a WSJ survey. The broad takeaway from the data is that households continue to spend and "that puts a floor under just how much U.K. growth can soften," Monex Europe analysts say in a note. Sterling rises to a high of $1.3502, according to FactSet, but is flat versus the euro, which last trades at 0.8409 pounds. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0559 ET - Over time, it may become politically desirable to reduce the U.S. deficit in order to bring long Treasury yields down and deliver support to mortgage rates, BlueBay CIO Mark Dowding says in a note. For now, this seems far off, however, he says. The U.S. administration has thrown down the gauntlet to the bond market and time will tell whether it attracts sufficient capital to stabilize yields, or whether the risks of a market tantrum could be on the rise, Dowding says. The 10-year Treasury yield is last down 3 basis points at 4.521%, while the 30-year yield falls 3.5 basis points at 5.029%, having hit an 18-month high of 5.161% on Thursday, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0459 ET - Two thirds of corporations surveyed by HSBC already experienced increases in costs due to the uncertainty around tariffs and trade, according to a survey by the global bank. Companies expect costs to increase further in both the short and long terms and forecast a revenue hit from supply chain delays. This is prompting more than three quarters of corporates to rethink their long-term business model. "The imperative for strategic adaptation is clear. Navigating this climate requires not only agility, but strong partnerships to ensure sustained growth in a shifting global economy," HSBC Global Trade Solutions' Vivek Ramachandran says. The survey draws from insight from 5,700 firms across 13 markets. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0356 ET - Yields on U.K. government bonds, or gilts, decline along with their eurozone and U.S. equivalents, after better-than-expected U.S. PMI data on Thursday and U.K. retail sales data on Friday revived investor optimism. Concerns over U.S. President Trump's tax-cut bill, which sent yields higher on Thursday, have ebbed for now. "One factor helping U.S. assets to stabilise was some stronger U.S. economic data, which continued to point away from a recession," Deutsche Bank Research analysts say in a note. The 10-year gilt yields falls around 3 basis points to last trade at 4.730%, Tradeweb data show. The 10-year Bund yields decline 3bps to 2.611%. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0352 ET - Gold is on track to post a weekly gain of more than 3%, supported by a softer U.S. dollar and concerns over the country' fiscal and economic outlook. In early trade, futures rise 1.1% to $3,331 a troy ounce, while the U.S. dollar index slips 0.5% to 99.45. "Gold is showing strength again, as it is particularly in demand in these politically uncertain times, also because other supposed safe havens such as the U.S. dollar are currently under pressure," says Barbara Lambrecht, commodity analyst at Commerzbank Research. Focus this week has shifted to the U.S. fiscal outlook after the House passed President Trump's tax-and-spending bill, which analysts say could significantly inflate the government deficit in the coming years. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0331 ET - Bitcoin eases slightly after hitting a fresh record high Thursday when the House passed President Trump's tax and spending bill. The fiscal plans have fuelled concerns about ballooning U.S. government debt following last week's credit rating downgrade by Moody's. This has encouraged investors to seek alternatives to the dollar, supporting bitcoin. Meanwhile, Trump held a private event Thursday night for investors of his meme coin. The Wall Street Journal reported that Trump reaffirmed his promise to make the U.S. the crypto capital of the world. Bitcoin falls 0.5% to $110,514 after reaching a high of $111,965 Thursday, according to LSEG. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0315 ET - The market likely needs more term premium--additional yield incentive to hold long-dated bonds rather than a short-dated bonds--for U.S. Treasurys to stabilize, Citi says in a global asset allocation view. "Even if the U.S. budget should not be as fiscally expensive as the more bearish observers suggest, the curve may stay steep." Given that U.S. year-on-year inflation is likely to rise, Citi sticks to an underweight position in U.S. Treasurys. However, the timing of a U.S. rates selloff is hard to predict, it says. The yield curve steepening--where the gap between short- and long-dated bond yields widens--has also been a global phenomenon, not just driven by U.S. Treasurys. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0251 ET - The dollar edges lower as worries over mounting U.S. government debt continue to weigh after the House passed Trump's tax and spending bill Thursday. The bill aims to make the tax cuts introduced by Trump permanent but the financing for this is shaky and it can be assumed that the budget deficit will continue to swell, Commerzbank currency analyst Antje Praefcke says in a note. "I am curious to see when the market will realize that this is the next major structural burden on the dollar," she says. The DXY dollar index falls 0.3% to 99.6710. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1834 ET [Dow Jones]--Ross Stores expects tariffs to put short-term pressure on its bottom line, CEO Jim Conroy says during the company's earnings call. Ross directly imports a small share of its products, but more than half of the total merchandise it sells originates in China, Conroy says. With recent tariffs threatening to raise prices on such goods, the company could see profit take a hit, and withdrew its full-year outlook due to such uncertainty, Conroy says. The company's vendors are working to shift their sourcing, he says, but it will likely take several months. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1507 ET [Dow Jones]--A lack of clarity on tariffs has pulled U.S. new-crop export sales down, CoBank said, adding the ongoing uncertainty is clouding the outlook for U.S. grain elevators and merchandisers. A research brief from CoBank's Knowledge Exchange said "the longer the uncertainty continues to drag on sales of new-crop exports, export-dependent grain elevators and merchandisers risk entering the 2025/26 marketing year with greater reliance on local demand, which may be scarce in some regions." As of May 1, U.S. new-crop export sales were below five-year historical averages, with soybeans falling 88.2% and corn dropping 26.9%. CoBank said. "Many foreign buyers have reduced forward coverage and are buying in the spot market, complicating the picture for elevators and merchandisers that are heavily reliant on export demand," CoBank said. CoBank is a cooperative bank serving industries across rural America. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1503 ET - Shifting U.S. trade policies, with tariffs on various partners still a threat, may slow the flow of export sales out of the U.S. this year, CoBank says in a note. Grain elevators, which buy grains from farmers to sell to domestic and foreign customers, are bracing for a smaller supply this year as farmers are unsure about more turmoil hitting crop prices. Farmers may opt to hold onto their harvested grains and not put them into the supply chain until that uncertainty eases. "For grain elevators and merchandisers that typically book grain sales out several months in advance, the headwinds of trade uncertainty remain firmly in place," the firm says. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1401 ET - New York Fed President John Williams says that central banks face a challenging set of questions when determining the quantity of financial reserves to provide to the banking system. The problem would be simple in a world where the banking sector demanded a stable level of reserves over time, and where the central bank isn't active as a lender of reserves, Williams says at a New York City conference. But in the real world, he says, solving for the right level of reserves is more complex. In general, uncertainty about the level of reserves demanded at any given time means central banks need to provide more reserves than they otherwise would, Williams suggests. "In other words, there is no single best way to supply reserves; rather, the best mix of tools depends on circumstances and policy preferences," Williams says. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

data.title: Cosunter Pharmaceutical's Unit Gets Nod to Market Ritonavir Tablets
data.description:
Fujian Cosunter Pharmaceutical's unit, Fujian Guangsheng Zhonglin Biotechnology, received the drug registration certificate from China's National Medical Products Administration to be allowed to market Ritonavir tablets, according to a Shenzhen Stock Exchange filing on Wednesday.

The drugs are allowed to be used in combination with other antiretroviral agents for the treatment of HIV-1 infection in adults and children aged 2 years and older, the pharmaceutical company said.
